content:
Type 2 Diabetes Management: HbA1c target less than 7% for most nonpregnant adults.
        Target less than 8% for older adults with multiple comorbidities or limited life expectancy.
        First-line pharmacotherapy is Metformin if eGFR above 30 and not contraindicated.
        Metformin mechanism: reduces hepatic glucose production via AMPK activation, improves
        peripheral insulin sensi

ivation, improves
        peripheral insulin sensitivity, mildly reduces intestinal glucose absorption.
        Does not cause hypoglycemia as monotherapy. Second-line therapy guided by comorbidities:
        ASCVD present: add GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) or SGLT2 inhibitor.
        Heart failure present: SGLT2 inhibitor preferred (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin).
        Weight loss priorit

lozin, dapagliflozin).
        Weight loss priority: GLP-1 RA preferred (semaglutide shows 15% body weight reduction).
        CKD present: SGLT2 inhibitor or GLP-1 RA.
        Early symptoms of Type 2 diabetes: polyuria (increased urination), polydipsia (increased thirst),
        polyphagia (increased hunger), fatigue, blurred vision, slow wound healing, 
        recurrent infections, tingling o

healing, 
        recurrent infections, tingling or numbness in hands and feet.
        Monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months until target achieved, then every 6 months.
        Annual screening: retinopathy, nephropathy (UACR and eGFR), neuropathy, foot exam.

Heart Failure Classification: HFrEF ejection fraction below 40%.
        HFmrEF ejection fraction 41 to 49%. HFpEF ejection fraction 50% or above.
        NYHA Classification: Class I no symptoms with ordinary activity. Class II slight limitation.
        Class III marked limitation. Class IV symptoms at rest.
        First-line treatment for HFrEF: ACE inhibitor or ARB plus beta-blocker plus

: ACE inhibitor or ARB plus beta-blocker plus
        mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA). SGLT2 inhibitors now recommended
        for both HFrEF and HFpEF regardless of diabetes status.
        Beta-blockers with mortality benefit: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol only.
        ICD indicated for EF below 35% with NYHA Class II or III on optimal medical therapy
        for at

or III on optimal medical therapy
        for at least 3 months. CRT indicated for LBBB with QRS above 150ms and EF below 35%.
        Acute decompensated heart failure: IV diuretics, vasodilators if hypertensive,
        inotropes for cardiogenic shock. BNP or NT-proBNP guides diagnosis and prognosis.

Hypertension Definition: Blood pressure above 130/80 mmHg per ACC/AHA 2017.
        Traditional definition above 140/90 mmHg. Stage 1: 130-139/80-89. Stage 2: above 140/90.
        Lifestyle modifications for all stages: DASH diet, sodium restriction below 2.3g per day,
        regular aerobic exercise 150 minutes per week, weight loss if overweight, limit alcohol.
        First-line pharmacothera

t, limit alcohol.
        First-line pharmacotherapy: ACE inhibitor or ARB, calcium channel blocker, or thiazide diuretic.
        Black patients: calcium channel blocker or thiazide diuretic preferred over ACEi or ARB
        due to lower efficacy of renin-angiotensin blockade in this population.
        CKD patients: ACE inhibitor or ARB regardless of race for renoprotection.
        Pregnancy:

ss of race for renoprotection.
        Pregnancy: labetalol, nifedipine, or methyldopa are safe antihypertensives.
        Avoid ACE inhibitors and ARBs in pregnancy due to fetal renal toxicity.
        Hypertensive emergency defined as BP above 180/120 with end-organ damage.
        Treatment: reduce MAP by no more than 25% in first hour using IV labetalol,
        nicardipine, or clevidipine. Do

labetalol,
        nicardipine, or clevidipine. Do not lower too rapidly.

Antibiotic safety during pregnancy: Several antibiotic classes are contraindicated.
        Fluoroquinolones including ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy.
        Mechanism of harm: fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV.
        In developing fetus, these enzymes are critical for cartilage development.
        Studies show fluoroquinolones cause arthrop

Studies show fluoroquinolones cause arthropathy and cartilage damage in animal models.
        Clinical concern: fetal joint and cartilage abnormalities.
        Also associated with increased risk of spontaneous abortion in some studies.
        Safe alternatives for UTI in pregnancy: nitrofurantoin (except at term due to hemolysis risk),
        cephalexin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, fosfom

cephalexin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, fosfomycin (single dose).
        Avoid in third trimester: nitrofurantoin (neonatal hemolysis), trimethoprim (folate antagonist).
        Tetracyclines including doxycycline: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy.
        Cause permanent tooth discoloration and bone growth inhibition in fetus.
        Aminoglycosides: use with caution, risk of fetal ototoxicity

ides: use with caution, risk of fetal ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
        Safe throughout pregnancy: penicillins, cephalosporins, azithromycin, clindamycin.

Acute Coronary Syndrome Recognition and Management:
        Classic STEMI symptoms: severe crushing chest pain radiating to left arm or jaw,
        diaphoresis (sweating), nausea, dyspnea, sense of impending doom.
        Any patient with chest pain plus dyspnea, diaphoresis, or radiation to arm or jaw
        should be treated as a cardiac emergency until proven otherwise.
        Immediate acti
source: Emergency Medicine Cardiac Chest Pain Protocol

content:
ncy until proven otherwise.
        Immediate actions for suspected MI: Call 911 immediately, do not drive self to hospital.
        Position patient comfortably, loosen tight clothing.
        Aspirin 325mg chewed immediately if not allergic and no contraindication.
        Do NOT give aspirin if patient has active bleeding, severe aspirin allergy, or recent GI bleed.
        STEMI management: pr
source: Emergency Medicine Cardiac Chest Pain Protocol

content:
, or recent GI bleed.
        STEMI management: primary PCI is gold standard, door to balloon time target under 90 minutes.
        Fibrinolysis if PCI not available within 120 minutes of first medical contact.
        Post-MI: dual antiplatelet therapy, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, high-intensity statin.
        Pulmonary embolism can mimic MI: pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, hypoxia, tachycardia.
source: Emergency Medicine Cardiac Chest Pain Protocol

content:
euritic chest pain, dyspnea, hypoxia, tachycardia.
        Aortic dissection: tearing pain radiating to back, pulse differential between arms.
source: Emergency Medicine Cardiac Chest Pain Protocol

content:
Depression Diagnosis: requires depressed mood or anhedonia plus at least 4 additional
        symptoms for minimum 2 weeks causing functional impairment.
        Additional symptoms: sleep disturbance, appetite changes, fatigue, concentration difficulties,
        psychomotor changes, worthlessness or excessive guilt, suicidal ideation.
        PHQ-9 screening tool: 0-4 minimal depression, 5-9 mil
source: WHO Depression and Mental Health Guidelines 2023

content:
-9 screening tool: 0-4 minimal depression, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate,
        15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe depression.
        Treatment: mild to moderate: psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) first line.
        Moderate to severe: antidepressants plus psychotherapy.
        SSRI first line: sertraline 50-200mg, escitalopram 10-20mg, fluoxetine 20-60mg.
        SNRI alternative: venlafaxine, duloxeti
source: WHO Depression and Mental Health Guidelines 2023

content:
g.
        SNRI alternative: venlafaxine, duloxetine.
        Onset of antidepressant effect: 2-4 weeks. Full effect: 6-8 weeks.
        Suicidal ideation or self-harm: immediate psychiatric evaluation required.
        Crisis resources: 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline (call or text, 24/7).
        Persistent hopelessness, feelings of worthlessness, not seeing point in continuing
        are serio
source: WHO Depression and Mental Health Guidelines 2023

content:
, not seeing point in continuing
        are serious symptoms requiring immediate professional evaluation.
        Do not dismiss or minimize these symptoms as normal stress.
source: WHO Depression and Mental Health Guidelines 2023

content:
Iron Poisoning in Children: One of most serious pediatric poisoning emergencies.
        Iron tablets are leading cause of pediatric poisoning death.
        Toxic dose: above 20mg per kg of elemental iron. Most adult tablets contain 65mg elemental iron.
        One adult iron tablet can be toxic to a 10kg (22 pound) child.
        Clinical stages of iron toxicity:
        Stage 1 (0-6 hours): nau

of iron toxicity:
        Stage 1 (0-6 hours): nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain. Child may appear well.
        Stage 2 (6-24 hours): apparent recovery period. Child looks better. DECEPTIVE PHASE.
        Stage 3 (12-48 hours): severe deterioration, metabolic acidosis, hepatotoxicity, shock.
        Stage 4 (2-5 days): hepatic failure, coagulopathy.
        Critical point: apparent recov

agulopathy.
        Critical point: apparent recovery in Stage 2 does not mean the child is safe.
        Many parents observe child looking fine after Stage 1 and do not seek care.
        This is a medical emergency regardless of current appearance.
        Immediate action: call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 and go to emergency room.
        Do not induce vomiting. Bring medication bottle to id

not induce vomiting. Bring medication bottle to identify exact elemental iron content.
        Treatment: gastric lavage if large ingestion, whole bowel irrigation,
        IV deferoxamine chelation therapy for severe cases.

Beta-blocker toxicity: metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol, propranolol overdose.
        Mechanism: excessive beta-1 and beta-2 blockade causing bradycardia and hypotension.
        Symptoms of beta-blocker overdose: bradycardia (heart rate below 60),
        hypotension, dizziness, fatigue, heart block on ECG, bronchospasm (non-selective agents).
        Propranolol additionally causes CNS toxicity

Propranolol additionally causes CNS toxicity and seizures due to membrane stabilizing effect.
        Double dosing of metoprolol 50mg: taking 100mg instead of 50mg.
        Effects may be delayed 4-6 hours with extended release formulations.
        Even with immediate release, significant bradycardia can develop within 1-2 hours.
        Action required: call Poison Control immediately at

tion required: call Poison Control immediately at 1-800-222-1222.
        Do not wait for symptoms. Monitoring and potential treatment needed.
        Treatment in hospital: IV atropine for bradycardia, high-dose insulin euglycemia therapy,
        IV calcium, IV glucagon, IV lipid emulsion for severe cases.
        Blood pressure medication and stopping treatment:
        Do NOT stop antihyperten

opping treatment:
        Do NOT stop antihypertensive medication because blood pressure is normal.
        Normal readings indicate medication is working effectively.
        Stopping abruptly causes rebound hypertension and increased cardiovascular risk.

Breast cancer screening: mammography every 2 years for women aged 50-74.
        Women 40-49: individualized decision based on values and risk factors.
        High risk patients: annual MRI plus mammography starting age 30.
        BRCA mutation carriers: prophylactic options discussion recommended.
        Colorectal cancer screening starting at age 45 for average risk adults.
        Options: c

age 45 for average risk adults.
        Options: colonoscopy every 10 years, annual FIT or gFOBT,
        stool DNA test every 1-3 years, CT colonography every 5 years.
        High risk (Lynch syndrome): colonoscopy every 1-2 years from age 20-25.
        Cervical cancer: Pap smear every 3 years for ages 21-65,
        or Pap plus HPV co-testing every 5 years for ages 30-65.
        Lung cancer:

very 5 years for ages 30-65.
        Lung cancer: annual low-dose CT for adults 50-80 with 20 pack-year smoking history
        who currently smoke or quit within past 15 years.
        Prostate cancer: shared decision-making with PSA testing from age 50,
        age 45 for African American men or family history, age 40 for very high risk.

CKD Staging by eGFR: G1 above 90 (normal or high), G2 60-89 (mildly decreased),
        G3a 45-59 (mildly to moderately decreased), G3b 30-44 (moderately to severely decreased),
        G4 15-29 (severely decreased), G5 below 15 (kidney failure).
        Albuminuria staging: A1 below 30 mg/g (normal), A2 30-300 mg/g (moderately increased),
        A3 above 300 mg/g (severely increased).
        Sl

A3 above 300 mg/g (severely increased).
        Slowing CKD progression: blood pressure target below 130/80 mmHg.
        ACE inhibitor or ARB for proteinuria regardless of blood pressure.
        SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce CKD progression
        and are now recommended for CKD with or without diabetes.
        Avoid NSAIDs in CKD due to renal toxicity.
        Metform

AIDs in CKD due to renal toxicity.
        Metformin: hold if eGFR 30-60 before iodinated contrast procedures.
        Contraindicated if eGFR below 30. Resume 48 hours after contrast if renal function stable.
        Dialysis or kidney transplant consideration for G5 CKD.
        Anemia management: erythropoiesis-stimulating agents if Hgb below 10 g/dL.
g/dL.
